perturbation.original_text: (b) Subject to Section 7(d), the Servicer may resign its duties hereunder by providing the Owner with at least 60 days' prior written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) Subject to Section 7(d), the Servicer may resign its duties hereunder by providing the Owner with at least 14 days' prior written notice.
perturbation.explanation: Reduced notice period for resignation may violate state laws requiring longer notification periods for certain types of service contracts.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware law regarding reasonable notice for contract termination
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law requires 'reasonable notification' before a contract can be terminated, allowing the affected party sufficient time to seek a replacement. A 14-day notice may be deemed unreasonable depending on the complexity of the services and the difficulty of finding a substitute servicer.

General Provisions Part 3 Territorial Applicability and General Rules Â§ 1-301. Territorial applicability; partiesâ power to choose applicable law. (a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this State and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this State or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), the Uniform Commercial Code applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) Section 2-402; (2) Sections 2A-105 and 2A-106; (3) Section 4-102; (4) Section 4A-507; (5) Section 5-116; (6) Section 8-110; (7) Sections 9-301 through 9-307; (8) Section 12-107. 5A Del. (b) The obligations of good faith, diligence, reasonableness, and care prescribed by the Uniform Commercial Code may not be disclaimed by agreement. The parties, by agreement, may determine the standards by which the performance of those obligations is to be measured if those standards ar
perturbation.justification: The original contract requires 60 days' notice for the Servicer to resign, while the changed text reduces this to 14 days. The Law Explanation suggests that Delaware law requires 'reasonable notification' for contract termination, allowing sufficient time to find a replacement. A 14-day notice period might be deemed unreasonable depending on the specifics of the services provided. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Section 1-302 (a) states, "Except as otherwise provided in subsection (b) or elsewhere in the Uniform Commercial Code, the effect of provisions of the Uniform Commercial Code may be varied by agreement." However, subsection (b) mentions "reasonableness," suggesting that variations must still adhere to this standard, supporting the claim that the reduced notice period could contradict the requirement for 'reasonable notification' and violate the standard of reasonableness required in Delaware law.

perturbation.original_text: Promptly upon the effective date of termination of this Agreement or the resignation or removal of the Servicer pursuant to Section 7, the Servicer shall be entitled to be paid all fees and reimbursable expenses, including any reasonable out-of-pocket attorneys' fees, accruing to it to the date of such termination, resignation or removal.
perturbation.changed_text: The Servicer shall be entitled to be paid all fees and reimbursable expenses, including any reasonable out-of-pocket attorneys' fees, accruing to it to the date of such termination, resignation or removal, sixty (60) days after the effective date of termination of this Agreement or the resignation or removal of the Servicer pursuant to Section 7.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying payment of final fees and expenses beyond a reasonable period could violate state wage payment laws or unjust enrichment principles.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY Labor Law § 191 mandates specific payment schedules for different types of employees, with stricter timelines upon termination. Delaying final payments for 60 days could violate these prompt payment requirements.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a 60-day delay in payment of fees and expenses, which contradicts the law explanation referencing NY Labor Law § 191 requiring prompt payment upon termination. Although the scraped snippet does not contain the specifics of this law, the Law Explanation explicitly states the prompt payment requirements, creating a contradiction with the changed contract's 60-day delay. The 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' definition covers time-sensitive obligations that violate state law.
